text: Photosynthesis is a system by which photopigment-bearing autotrophic organisms convert light energy into chemical energy for metabolism.
Oxygenic photosynthesis, the most common type, releases oxygen as a byproduct of water splitting.
Photosynthetic organisms store converted chemical energy in intracellular organic compounds, typically carbohydrates like glucose and sucrose.
Purple bacteria use bacteriochlorophyll to split hydrogen sulfide, releasing sulfur instead of oxygen as byproduct.
In plants, algae, and cyanobacteria, the Calvin cycle incorporates atmospheric carbon dioxide into organic compounds.
Cyanobacteria produced excess oxygen that oxygenated Earth, enabling evolution of complex life.
Global photosynthesis captures approximately 130 terawatts of energy, about eight times total human power consumption.
Photosynthesis occurs in chloroplasts in plants and algae, but in photosynthetic bacterial cell membranes.
Light-dependent reactions produce NADPH and ATP used by light-independent reactions to reduce carbon dioxide.
In light-dependent reactions, electrons move through transport chains generating a proton gradient for ATP synthesis.
Water photolysis in light-dependent reactions releases oxygen and provides electrons replenishing chlorophyll molecules.
Chlorophyll reflects green light, explaining why most plants appear green.
Some organisms use non-water electron donors; some archaea use bacteriorhodopsin without releasing oxygen.
Leaf mesophyll cells contain 450,000 to 800,000 chloroplasts per square millimeter of tissue.
Not all light wavelengths support photosynthesis; effectiveness depends on the organism's pigment type.
Light-dependent reactions occur in thylakoid membranes and synthesize ATP and NADPH.
In non-cyclic reactions, photosystem II absorbs photons, exciting electrons through the electron transport chain.
Proton pumping across the thylakoid membrane generates chemiosmotic potential that ATP synthase uses to synthesize ATP.
Two water molecules are oxidized by photosystem II, yielding one oxygen molecule and four hydrogen ions.
Cyclic reactions occur only at photosystem I and produce ATP but not NADPH.
RuBisCO fixes atmospheric CO2 during the Calvin cycle to produce three-carbon sugar carbohydrates.
Carbon dioxide combines with ribulose 1,5-bisphosphate yielding glycerate 3-phosphate molecules.
Glycerate 3-phosphate reduces to glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate using ATP and NADPH.
Glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate regenerates ribulose 1,5-bisphosphate or condenses into hexose phosphates forming sugars.
C4 plants fix CO2 in mesophyll cells via PEP carboxylase producing oxaloacetic acid.
C4 plants concentrate CO2 in bundle sheath cells, reducing photorespiration versus C3 plants.
CAM plants separate CO2 fixation at night from Calvin cycle during the day.
Cyanobacteria use carboxysomes with carbonic anhydrase to concentrate CO2 around RuBisCO.
Over 90% of plants employ C3 carbon fixation; approximately 3% use C4.
Calcium-oxalate crystals in alarm photosynthesis function as dynamic internal carbon sources.
Plants convert light energy into chemical energy at photosynthetic efficiency of 3–6%.
Unconverted absorbed light is dissipated primarily as heat, with 1–2% reemitted as chlorophyll fluorescence.
Plants' photosynthetic efficiency varies with light frequency, intensity, temperature, and CO2 proportion, ranging 0.1% to 8%.
Quantum walk increases light energy transport efficiency in photosynthetic cells by enabling excitons to test multiple paths.
Filamentous photosynthetic organism fossils date to 3.4 billion years old.
Thylakoid membranes in 1.75-billion-year-old cherts provide the first direct evidence of photosynthesis.
Oxygenic photosynthesis is the main source of oxygen in Earth's atmosphere.
Oxygenic photosynthesis became important in cyanobacteria during the Paleoproterozoic era, approximately two billion years ago.
Corals, sponges, and sea anemones form symbiotic relationships with photosynthetic algae.
Elysia viridis and Elysia chlorotica capture chloroplasts from algae and survive through photosynthesis alone for months.
Chloroplasts possess DNA separate from plant nuclear DNA, with genes resembling those found in cyanobacteria.
Endosymbiotic theory proposes early eukaryotic cells engulfed photosynthetic bacteria to form the first plant cells.
Archaeplastida and Paulinella acquired two-membrane plastids through primary endosymbiosis events with cyanobacteria.
Other photosynthetic eukaryotes acquired plastids from red or green algae, forming distinct red and green lineages.
Cyanobacteria are the only oxygenic photosynthetic prokaryotes, possessing both Type I and Type II photosystems.
Water-use in photosynthesis evolved in a cyanobacteria common ancestor at least 2450–2320 million years ago.
Cyanobacteria remained the principal primary producers of oxygen throughout the Proterozoic Eon (2500–543 million years ago).
Jan van Helmont demonstrated that most plant mass comes from water, not solely from soil.
Joseph Priestley discovered that plants could restore air that was injured by burning candles and animal respiration.
Jan Ingenhousz (1779) discovered that sunlight influences a plant's ability to revive injured air within hours.
Jean Senebier (1796) demonstrated that green plants consume carbon dioxide and release oxygen under light.
Robert Emerson discovered two distinct light reactions in photosynthesis by testing different light wavelengths.
Samuel Ruben and Martin Kamen used radioactive isotopes to determine that oxygen liberated in photosynthesis comes from water.
Rudolph A. Marcus discovered the function and significance of the electron transport chain.
In 1950, Otto Kandler presented first experimental evidence for photophosphorylation in vivo using Chlorella.
In 1954, Daniel I. Arnon et al. discovered photophosphorylation in vitro in isolated chloroplasts.
Louis N. M. Duysens and Jan Amesz discovered two light reactions occurring in series.
Melvin Calvin won 1961 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for photosynthetic carbon metabolism research.
Field-grown maize showed leaf photosynthetic rates of 40 μmol CO2·m−2·s−1, nearly double rates in wheat and soybean.
C4 plants show no photorespiration and photosynthetic rates never saturate at full sunlight.
At constant temperature, carbon assimilation rate increases with light irradiance until reaching saturation.
Temperature affects carbon assimilation rate, revealing light-dependent temperature-independent and light-independent temperature-dependent reaction stages.
Wavelength of light is a limiting factor; cyanobacteria use Phycobilisome light-harvesting complex to capture wavelengths underwater.
Phycobilisome consists of proteins with different pigments arranged around a reaction center.
RuBisCO binds carbon dioxide when concentration is high but binds oxygen when carbon dioxide concentration is low.
When RuBisCO binds oxygen, photorespiration produces phosphoglycolate instead of 3-phosphoglycerate, consuming energy without making sugars.
Phosphoglycolate converts to glycolate, which is toxic to plants at high concentration and inhibits photosynthesis.
The glycolate salvaging pathway returns only 75% of carbon to the Calvin-Benson cycle and produces ammonia that plants lose.
The Eiffel Tower is a lattice tower on the Champ de Mars in Paris, France.
Maurice Koechlin and Émile Nouguier designed the tower; Gustave Eiffel's company built it from 1887 to 1889.
The tower was constructed as the centrepiece of the 1889 Exposition Universelle, celebrating the French Revolution's centennial.
The tower is locally nicknamed 'La dame de fer' (Iron Lady) for its use of wrought iron.
The tower was initially criticised but became a global cultural icon and one of the world's most recognisable structures.
The Eiffel Tower is 330 metres tall, the tallest structure in Paris, and was the world's tallest human-made structure for 41 years.
The tower is the most visited monument with entrance fees in the world, receiving 5.89 million visitors in 2022.
The tower has three visitor levels with restaurants on the first two and a private apartment on the third level built for Gustave Eiffel.
Crédit Industriel et Commercial (CIC) helped finance construction of the Eiffel Tower.
CIC acquired funds from predatory loans to Haiti's National Bank for tower financing.
France forced Haiti to financially compensate French slaveowners for losses in the Haitian Revolution.
Haiti was required to pay CIC and partner nearly half of all export taxes collected.
Prior to Eiffel Tower construction, no structure had been built to 300 m height.
Committee of Three Hundred, led by architect Charles Garnier, protested Eiffel Tower construction.
An 'Artists against the Eiffel Tower' petition was published in Le Temps on February 14, 1887.
By 1918, the tower became a symbol of Paris after Apollinaire's nationalist calligram poem.
Guy de Maupassant ate lunch daily in the tower restaurant to avoid seeing the tower.
Each shoe was anchored by a pair of bolts 10 cm diameter and 7.5 m long
Foundations were completed on 30 June; ironwork erection then began
The drawing office produced 1,700 general and 3,629 detailed drawings of 18,038 parts
Rivet hole position was specified to within 1 mm; angles to one second of arc
Components arrived on horse-drawn carts from Levallois-Perret factory near Paris
18,038 pieces were joined using 2.5 million rivets
Initially legs were constructed as cantilevers; halfway up, construction paused for timber scaffold
Charles Garnier called the tower a 'truly tragic street lamp'
A 'creeper' crane was installed in each leg to move up the tower
Hydraulic jacks at shoe base could exert 800 tonnes of force
Main structural work was completed end of March 1889
On 31 March, Gustave Eiffel led government officials and press to the tower top
At 2:35 pm on 31 March, Eiffel hoisted a Tricolour flag with a 25-gun salute
The tower opened to the public 9 days after exposition opening on 6 May
Nearly 30,000 visitors climbed 1,710 steps to the top before lifts entered service on 26 May
Ticket prices: 2 francs first level, 3 second, 5 top; half-price Sundays
By exhibition end, 1,896,987 visitors had visited the tower
The tower was lit by hundreds of gas lamps; a beacon sent red, white, blue light
Le Figaro newspaper operated an office and printing press on the second level
A post office at the top allowed visitors to send letters and postcards
Thomas Edison visited and gave Eiffel a phonograph in his private apartment at tower top
Edison signed the guestbook on 10 September 1889
Eiffel used his tower apartment for meteorological observations
The tower was used for experiments on air resistance on falling bodies
Eiffel's permit allowed the tower to stand for 20 years until its planned dismantlement in 1909.
The tower remained standing after its permit expired due to its value for radio telegraphy.
In 1901, Alberto Santos-Dumont won 100,000 francs flying from St. Cloud to the tower and back in under 30 minutes.
Father Theodor Wulf discovered cosmic rays in 1910 by measuring radiant energy at the tower.
Franz Reichelt, an Austrian tailor, died on 4 February 1912 jumping from the first level to test his parachute.
The tower's 1914 radio transmitter jammed German communications, contributing to the Battle of the Marne victory.
Victor Lustig fraudulently 'sold' the Eiffel Tower for scrap metal on two separate occasions in 1925.
From 1925 to 1934, illuminated Citroën signs adorned three tower sides, making it the world's tallest advertising space.
In 1944, Hitler ordered General von Choltitz to demolish the tower during German occupation, but he refused.
In 1967, Montreal Mayor Jean Drapeau negotiated with Charles de Gaulle for temporary tower relocation to Montreal for Expo 67, vetoed by the operating company.
In 1982, 97-year-old lifts between second and third levels were replaced with new cars reducing journey time from eight to under two minutes.
Original lifts closed to public November-March due to hydraulic water freezing.
Robert Moriarty flew a Beechcraft Bonanza under the tower on 31 March 1984.
A. J. Hackett bungee jumped from the tower's top in 1987 and was arrested.
For Y2K on 31 December 1999, flashing lights and searchlights were installed; sparkling lighting continued 18 months until July 2001.
The tower's puddle iron structure weighs 7,300 tonnes; total weight with equipment is approximately 10,100 tonnes.
The tower's top shifts up to 18 centimeters due to thermal expansion, and the tower sways up to 9 centimeters in wind.
Tower columns contain stairs and lifts to first two floors, with only south column's second-floor lift publicly accessible
First floor originally housed three restaurants (French, Russian, Flemish) and Anglo-American Bar, later replaced by Le 58 Tour Eiffel
Le Jules Verne gourmet restaurant on second floor has one Michelin star; Alain Ducasse managed it 2007-2017, Frédéric Anton as of May 2019
Third floor originally contained laboratories and Gustave Eiffel's apartment, now open to public with period furnishings and mannequins
Restaurant atop tower operated from 1937-1981 and was dismantled due to structural weight; rebuilt in New Orleans' Garden District
Modern lifts average 8 minutes 50 seconds per round trip and spend approximately 1 minute 15 seconds at each stop
Roux, Combaluzier & Lepape supplied two lifts to tower's east and west legs using endless chains with articulated links
Otis Brothers & Company contracted to build second-level lifts in July 1887 after French companies refused the work
Gustave Eiffel engraved the names of 72 French scientists, engineers, and mathematicians on the tower.
The tower is painted in three shades: lighter at top, progressively darker toward bottom.
The Eiffel Tower changed from reddish brown to 'Eiffel Tower Brown' in 1968 and yellow-brown in 2010.
Tower maintenance requires 60 tons of paint every 7 years to prevent rusting.
The Eiffel Tower was the world's tallest structure from 1889 until the Chrysler Building surpassed it in 1929.
More than 300 million people have visited the Eiffel Tower since its completion in 1889.
The Eiffel Tower is the world's most-visited paid monument with approximately 25,000 daily visitors.
The Eiffel Tower's illumination is protected by copyright as an 'original visual creation' under French law.
The copyright claim regarding the Eiffel Tower has never been tested in court, according to a 2014 Art Law Journal article.
Commercial uses of Eiffel Tower images in magazines, film posters, or product packaging may require prior permission.
Pierre Bideau, who created the 1989 lighting display, died in 2021; the display copyright remains protected until 2091.
Blackpool Tower in England was commissioned after the 1889 exposition, opened in 1894, and is 158.1 meters tall.
Tokyo Tower in Japan was built in 1958 as a communications tower inspired by the Eiffel Tower.
Eiffel Tower replicas exist worldwide including versions in Paris Las Vegas, Paris Texas, amusement parks in Ohio and Virginia, and in China, Mexico, and Europe.
A 2011 National Geographic television program estimated a full-size Eiffel Tower replica would cost approximately US$480 million to build.
The original Eiffel Tower cost nearly 8 million francs in 1890, approximately US$40 million in 2018 dollars.
Jupiter is the fifth planet from the Sun and the largest planet in the Solar System.
Jupiter's atmosphere consists of 76% hydrogen and 24% helium by mass.
Jupiter rotates once every ten hours, making it an oblate spheroid with equatorial radius 7% larger than polar radius.
The Great Red Spot is a giant storm on Jupiter recorded since 1831.
At least 115 moons orbit Jupiter; the four largest—Io, Europa, Ganymede, and Callisto—are visible with common binoculars.
Jupiter has a faint ring system consisting mainly of dust with three main segments: halo, main ring, and gossamer ring.
Jupiter formed as the oldest planet in the Solar System, approximately 1 million years after the Sun.
According to the grand tack hypothesis, Jupiter migrated inward then outward, allowing inner planets including Earth to form.
The grand tack hypothesis has unresolved issues regarding terrestrial planet formation timescales and elemental composition.
The Nice model proposes proto-Kuiper belt objects caused Jupiter and Saturn to migrate into 1:2 resonance, disrupting Uranus and Neptune.
The Jumping-Jupiter scenario suggests Jupiter's migration could have ejected a fifth gas giant from the Solar System.
Jupiter formed beyond the nitrogen snow line at 20-30 AU and migrated inward over 700,000 years to its current location.
Jupiter is 11 times wider than Earth with mass 318 times Earth's and 2.5 times all other planets combined.
Jupiter's atmosphere contains 76% hydrogen and 24% helium by mass, with trace ammonia, water vapor, and hydrocarbons.
Jupiter radiates more heat than it receives from solar radiation, causing approximately 1 mm shrinkage annually through Kelvin-Helmholtz mechanism.
If Jupiter had over 40% more mass, its interior would compress despite added matter.
Jupiter's axial tilt is 3.13 degrees, so its seasons are insignificant.
Jupiter rotates fastest of all Solar System planets, completing rotation in less than ten hours.
Jupiter's equatorial radius is 7% longer than its polar radius.
Jupiter has a diffuse core extending 30-50% of radius with 7-25 Earth masses of heavy elements.
Jupiter's atmosphere is primarily molecular hydrogen and helium with water, methane, hydrogen sulfide, and ammonia.
Jupiter is perpetually covered with ammonia crystal clouds forming bands at different latitudes.
Jupiter's cloud layer is approximately 50 km deep with at least two ammonia cloud decks.
Water clouds underlying Jupiter's ammonia clouds generate thunderstorms with lightning 1,000 times stronger than Earth lightning.
Jupiter's cloud colors derive from phosphorus, sulfur, or hydrocarbons exposed to ultraviolet radiation.
The Great Red Spot is a persistent anticyclonic storm larger than Earth located 22° south of equator.
The Great Red Spot has decreased from 41,000 km across in late 1800s to 16,500 by 10,940 km by 2015.
Jupiter has the strongest magnetic field in the Solar System, generated by metallic hydrogen core eddy currents.
Jupiter's magnetosphere extends nearly to Saturn's orbit and shields its four largest moons from solar wind.
Io's volcanic emissions of sulfur dioxide form a gas torus that becomes ionized in Jupiter's magnetosphere
Electrons in Jupiter's plasma sheet generate radio bursts in 0.6–30 MHz range detectable from Earth with shortwave receivers
Jupiter's barycenter with the Sun lies outside the Sun's volume by seven percent of Sun's radius
Jupiter orbits the Sun every 11.86 years at an average distance of 778 million kilometers
Jupiter's faint ring system is composed of dust material originating from the planet's small moons
Jupiter has 111 known natural satellites, with four largest being Galilean moons Ganymede, Callisto, Io, and Europa
Io, Europa, and Ganymede form a Laplace orbital resonance where tidal flexing generates internal heat
Jupiter's moons were classified into four groups of four based on similar orbital elements.
The eight innermost Jupiter moons have nearly circular equatorial orbits and formed alongside Jupiter.
Jupiter's irregular moons are likely captured asteroids or asteroid fragments.
Jupiter's gravity caused most Kirkwood gaps in the asteroid belt.
Jupiter experiences approximately 200 times more asteroid and comet impacts than Earth.
Jupiter family comets originate in the Kuiper belt beyond Neptune's orbit.
Babylonian astronomers observed Jupiter during the 7th-8th centuries BC.
Galileo discovered Jupiter's four largest Galilean moons in 1610 using a telescope.
Francesco Fontana discovered Jupiter's atmospheric bands using a 22-palm telescope in autumn 1639.
Giovanni Cassini observed Jupiter's atmospheric spots and oblate shape during the 1660s.
Cassini discovered differential rotation of Jupiter's atmosphere in 1692.
Ole Rømer deduced light does not travel instantaneously using Jupiter moon observation timing discrepancies.
Rupert Wildt identified ammonia and methane absorption bands in Jupiter's spectrum in 1932.
Bernard Burke and Kenneth Franklin discovered Jupiter emits radio bursts at 22.2 MHz in 1955.
Pioneer 10 became the first spacecraft to pass close to Jupiter in 1973.
Voyager missions discovered Jupiter's rings and confirmed the Great Red Spot is anticyclonic.
Galileo was the first spacecraft to orbit Jupiter, arriving December 7, 1995, and remaining in orbit for over seven years.
A 340-kilogram titanium atmospheric probe from Galileo entered Jupiter's atmosphere December 7, 1995, collecting data for 57.6 minutes.
NASA deliberately steered Galileo into Jupiter on September 21, 2003, to avoid contaminating the moon Europa.
Hydrogen composes up to 90% of Jupiter's atmosphere; recorded temperatures exceeded 300 °C and wind speeds exceeded 644 km/h.
NASA's Juno mission arrived at Jupiter on July 4, 2016, to study the planet from a polar orbit.
Juno's mission was extended to September 2025 with planned flybys of Ganymede, Europa, and Io.
Jupiter Icy Moon Explorer launched April 14, 2023; NASA's Europa Clipper launched October 14, 2024.
Hindu astrologers in Vedic astrology named Jupiter after Brihaspati, the gods' religious teacher, calling it Guru
Central Asian Turkic peoples called Jupiter Erendiz or Erentüz in their mythology
Turks calculated Jupiter's orbital period as 11 years and 300 days
Turks believed social and natural events correlated with Jupiter's movements in the sky
Chinese, Japanese, Korean, and Vietnamese cultures called Jupiter the wood star based on Five Elements
Chinese astronomers called Jupiter the year star because it moved one zodiac constellation annually
Ancient Chinese writings named years in correlation with Jupiter's position in the Jovian zodiac
Johann Sebastian Bach was born 31 March 1685 and died 28 July 1750.
Bach was a German composer and musician of the late Baroque period.
Bach is known for numerous works including Brandenburg Concertos, Cello Suites, Goldberg Variations, and St. Matthew Passion.
Felix Mendelssohn precipitated the Bach Revival by performing St. Matthew Passion in 1829.
Bach was born in Eisenach as the youngest child of musician Johann Ambrosius Bach.
By 1723 Bach became Thomaskantor and church music director of Leipzig.
Four of Bach's twenty children became composers: Wilhelm Friedemann, Carl Philipp Emanuel, Johann Christoph Friedrich, and Johann Christian.
Bach's mother died in 1694 and his father died eight months later in February 1695.
Ten-year-old Bach moved in with his eldest brother Johann Christoph Bach, organist at St. Michael's Church.
Johann Christoph taught Bach clavichord and exposed him to compositions by Pachelbel, Buxtehude, Lully, and other major composers.
On 3 April 1700, Bach began studies at St Michael's School in Lüneburg.
On 14 August 1703, Bach became organist at the New Church in Arnstadt.
In 1705-1706, Bach took extended leave from Arnstadt to study with organists Reincken and Buxtehude in Lübeck.
In 1707, Bach married his second cousin Maria Barbara Bach.
Maria Barbara Bach died suddenly on 7 July 1720 while Bach was in Carlsbad.
In 1721, Bach married Anna Magdalena Wilcke, a soprano sixteen years his junior.
Bach returned to Weimar in 1708 as court organist after Duke Johann Ernst III died.
Bach left Mühlhausen in 1708 and returned to Weimar as organist, becoming Konzertmeister from 1714.
In Weimar, Bach composed keyboard and orchestral works, transcribing Italian concertos for harpsichord and organ.
Bach began writing preludes and fugues in Weimar that were later assembled into The Well-Tempered Clavier.
In 1717, Bach fell out of favor in Weimar and was jailed for approximately one month before dismissal.
Leopold, Prince of Anhalt-Köthen hired Bach as Kapellmeister in 1717, a position that allowed secular composing.
In 1723, Bach was appointed Thomaskantor in Leipzig, directing church music for four churches for 27 years.
Bach was Thomaskantor and director musices in Leipzig from 1723 to 1729.
Bach composed new works for almost all liturgical occasions in his first year in Leipzig.
Bach's second-year chorale cantata cycle used hymns selected according to prescribed readings and ministerial plans.
The St Matthew Passion was first performed on Good Friday, 11 April 1727.
Bach composed the Christmas Oratorio in 1734-35 using previously composed works.
Bach received the title of Royal Court Composer from Augustus III of Poland in 1736.
The Goldberg Variations, published in 1741, contains nine canons in two-manual harpsichord arrangement.
Bach's final decade incorporated stile antico elements including polyphonic structures and canons.
In May 1747, Bach visited King Frederick the Great's court in Potsdam and improvised a three-part fugue.
Bach composed The Musical Offering based on King Frederick the Great's theme and dedicated The Musical Offering to Frederick.
Bach expanded a 1733 Kyrie-Gloria Mass into the Mass in B minor during the last years of his life.
The Mass in B minor, not performed during Bach's lifetime, is considered among the greatest choral works in history.
Bach died on 28 July 1750 from complications due to unsuccessful eye surgery.
Though Bach never visited France or Italy, French and Italian musical influences appear evident in Bach's compositions.
Bach transcribed six of Vivaldi's concerti for solo keyboard, three for organ, and one for four harpsichords, strings, and basso continuo.
Bach used the first movement theme of Vivaldi's Spring concerto from The Four Seasons in his cantata Wer weiß, wie nahe mir mein Ende? (BWV 27).
Arcangelo Corelli's chamber music influenced an entire generation of composers including Bach, Vivaldi, Handel, and Couperin.
Jean-Baptiste Lully invented the French overture in the 1650s, which Bach and Handel used extensively in their compositions.
Bach's hundreds of sacred works manifest his deep Christian faith expressed through elaboration of Lutheran chorale hymns.
Bach's compositional style centered on four-part harmony, which replaced modal music during the tonal system's rise in his era.
Bach returned to antiquated modes and genres more frequently than his contemporaries, despite championing the tonal system.
Bach pushed modulation beyond contemporary limits; Louis Marchand avoided confrontation with Bach because Bach exceeded everyone's modulation experiments.
Bach was a significant contributor to developing temperament for keyboard instruments allowing use in all keys without retuning.
The Well-Tempered Clavier, using all keys, represents the full expansion of Bach's temperament innovations from approximately 1720 onward.
Bach elevated basso continuo instruments from accompaniment to concertante (solo) roles, pioneering the keyboard concerto genre.
Bach composed virtuoso works for specific instruments and compositions without specified instrumentation, allowing easy transcription and reperformance.
Bach's style is characterized by extensive counterpoint, particularly in canons and fugues, which he did not invent but fundamentally developed.
A fugue is a contrapuntal compositional technique in two or more voices built on a musical subject introduced in imitation.
Fugues typically open with the subject sounding successively in each voice, followed by episodes and entries in related keys before returning to the tonic.
Bach devoted more attention to composition structure than his contemporaries, evidenced by adjustments to adapted works and revisions of his own compositions.
Librettos played an essential role in Bach's vocal compositions, with collaborations including Picander for the St Matthew Passion libretto.
Throughout his career, Bach copied and arranged compositions by other composers and his own music for learning, performance, and study material.
The Bach-Werke-Verzeichnis, established in 1950, catalogued spurious and doubtful works in an appendix (Anhang, abbreviated Anh.).
After Bach's death, his reputation as a composer initially declined as his work was seen as old-fashioned.
Carl Philipp Emanuel Bach actively safeguarded his father's legacy through multiple efforts including manuscript preservation.
Abraham Mendelssohn purchased Bach manuscripts from C.P.E. Bach's inheritance in 1805 and donated them to Berlin Sing-Akademie.
Haydn, Mozart, and Beethoven collected, transcribed, and were influenced by Johann Sebastian Bach's compositions.
Gottfried van Swieten, an Austrian official, was instrumental in transmitting Johann Sebastian Bach's legacy to Viennese school composers.
Johann Nikolaus Forkel published the first Bach biography in 1802, dedicated to van Swieten.
Abraham Mendelssohn acquired Bach manuscripts from C.P.E. Bach's heirs in 1805 and donated them to the Berlin Sing-Akademie.
Felix Mendelssohn's 1829 performance of the St. Matthew Passion initiated the Bach Revival.
The Bach-Gesellschaft was founded in 1850 to promote Bach's music and publish his complete works.
Glenn Gould's 1955 recording of the Goldberg Variations transformed the work from obscure to standard piano repertoire.
By the end of the 19th century, Bach was firmly established as one of the greatest composers in Western music.
Nikolaus Harnoncourt acquired prominence performing Bach on harpsichord and Baroque instruments with ensembles sized to Bach's original practice.
Ferruccio Busoni created late-romantic Bach-Busoni piano editions and Leopold Stokowski orchestrated Bach's Toccata and Fugue in D minor for Fantasia.
Jazz musicians including Jacques Loussier, Ian Anderson, and Uri Caine created jazz adaptations of Bach's works.
20th-century composers Eugène Ysaÿe, Dmitri Shostakovich, and Heitor Villa-Lobos composed works referencing or inspired by Bach.
Bach's Brandenburg Concerto No. 2, Partita for Violin No. 3, and Well-Tempered Clavier Prelude No. 1 are on Voyager Golden Records.
In the 21st century, Bach's compositions became available online through the International Music Score Library Project and Bach Digital website.
In 2015, Bach's handwritten copy of the Mass in B minor was added to UNESCO's Memory of the World Register.
Carol K. Baron published Bach's Changing World: Voices in the Community in 2006.
Alfred Dörffel published Thematisches Verzeichnis der Instrumentalwerke von Joh. Seb. Bach in 1882.
Wolfgang Schmieder's complete thematic catalogue superseded Dörffel's catalogue for scholarly purposes.
Douglas Hofstadter published Gödel, Escher, Bach in 1999, originally published in 1979.
Hofstadter's book uses Escher's art, Gödel's logic, and Bach's music to illustrate strange loops.
Steven Isserlis published The Bach Cello Suites: A Companion in 2021.
Mount Everest is Earth's highest mountain above sea level, lying in the Mahalangur Himal sub-range of the Himalayas.
Mount Everest is known as Sagarmāthā in Nepal, meaning 'goddess of the sky,' and Qomolangma in Tibet, meaning 'holy mother.'
Mount Everest's height was measured in 2020 by Chinese and Nepali authorities as 8,848.86 m (29,031 ft).
Mount Everest has two main climbing routes: one from the southeast in Nepal and one from the north in Tibet.
As of May 2024, 340 people have died on Mount Everest; over 200 bodies remain on the mountain.
Tenzing Norgay and Edmund Hillary made the first documented ascent of Mount Everest in 1953 using the Southeast Ridge route.
In 1802, the British began the Great Trigonometrical Survey of India to determine locations, heights, and names of the world's highest mountains.
Nepal refused British surveyors entry, forcing observations from Terai, a region south of Nepal parallel to the Himalayas.
In 1849, James Nicolson made over 30 observations of peak 'b' from five different locations, closest being 174 km away.
In 1852, Radhanath Sikdar identified peak 'b' as Earth's highest mountain using trigonometric calculations based on Nicolson's measurements.
Andrew Waugh announced in March 1856 that Peak XV measured 8,840 m after several years of verification calculations.
Nepal and China officially recognized Mount Everest's height as 8,848 m based on modern survey measurements.
On 9 October 2005, the Chinese Academy of Sciences announced Mount Everest's height as 8,844.43 m, based on rock height not snow.
China and Nepal disputed whether Mount Everest's official height should be rock height of 8,844 m or snow height of 8,848 m.
On 8 December 2020, China and Nepal jointly announced Mount Everest's official height as 8,848.86 metres (29,031.7 ft).
The Main Himalayan Thrust and related faults are adding height to Mount Everest and moving its summit northeastwards.
Mauna Kea in Hawaii rises over 10,200 m from its mid-ocean floor base but only 4,205 m above sea level.
Mount Everest's rocks are subdivided into three formations—Qomolangma, North Col, and Rongbuk—separated by low-angle faults.
North Col Formation between 7,000–8,200 m on Mount Everest contains schist, phyllite, and minor marble.
Rongbuk Formation underlying North Col Formation consists of sillimanite-K-feldspar schist, gneiss, and leucogranite intrusions.
Leucogranites in Rongbuk Formation formed 20–24 million years ago from partial melting during Indian Plate subduction.
Mount Everest is rising approximately 2 millimeters per year.
Minute black jumping spiders of genus Euophrys live at 6,700 metres on Mount Everest.
Moss species grows at 6,480 metres on Mount Everest, possibly the highest altitude plant.
Everest base camp by Khumbu Glacier is destabilizing due to climate change; planned relocation to lower altitude was put on hold.
Mount Everest's summit extends into the stratosphere with air pressure one-third of sea level; recorded wind speeds reached 280 km/h in February 2004.
The Balcony Station, highest weather station on Earth at 8,430 meters, was established by Tom Matthews and Baker Perry in 2019.
Mount Everest was first confirmed summited in 1953; as of December 2024, 13,000 summits by 7,200 climbers were recorded.
George Mallory and Andrew Irvine disappeared attempting Everest's summit on June 8, 1924; Mallory's body was found in 1999.
George Mallory and Guy Bullock discovered the northern approach to Mount Everest during the 1921 British Reconnaissance Expedition.
Bill Tilman's 1950 exploratory expedition through Nepal established the standard south approach to Everest.
Raymond Lambert and Tenzing Norgay reached 8,595 m on the 1952 Swiss expedition, setting a new climbing altitude record.
Edmund Hillary and Tenzing Norgay reached Mount Everest's summit at 11:30 am on 29 May 1953 via the South Col route.
Junko Tabei became the first woman to summit Everest in 1975.
Reinhold Messner and Peter Habeler made the first ascent of Everest without supplemental oxygen in 1978.
Leszek Cichy and Krzysztof Wielicki completed the first winter ascent of Everest on 17 February 1980.
Eight climbers died in a blizzard on 10-11 May 1996 during a summit attempt on Mount Everest.
Fifteen people died during the 1996 Mount Everest season, the highest death toll until 16 deaths in 2014.
Jon Krakauer published the bestseller Into Thin Air about his experience in one affected guided party.
Beck Weathers survived Mount Everest despite being left for dead 275 metres from Camp 4 at 7,950 metres.
David Sharp died during a 2006 Mount Everest climb, sparking debate about climbing ethics and rescue obligations.
Meagan McGrath conducted a successful high-altitude rescue of Nepali Usha Bista on 21 May 2007 on Mount Everest.
By end of 2010, Mount Everest had 5,104 summits by about 3,142 individuals; 77% occurred since 2000.
1996 Mount Everest disaster was partly caused by bottleneck of 33-36 climbers attempting summit on same day.
On 18 April 2014, avalanche below Base Camp 2 killed 16 Nepali guides and injured 9 people.
25 April 2015 earthquake measuring 7.8 Mw triggered avalanche at Everest Base Camp; 2015 was first year since 1974 without spring summits.
12 climbers died on Mount Everest during spring 2019 season; 891 summited despite high death toll.
Nepal and China prohibited foreign climbing on Mount Everest in 2020 due to COVID-19 pandemic.
In October 2025, blizzard-like conditions prompted Chinese authorities to evacuate nearly 350 people, with about 200 more stranded on Everest Scenic Area.
In 2021, Nepal issued 408 climbing permits, setting a record number.
In April 2025, Nepal required climbers to have summited a 7,000-metre peak within Nepal before obtaining an Everest permit, effective Spring 2026.
According to Jon Krakauer, commercialization of Everest began in 1985 with a guided expedition led by David Breashears that included Richard Bass.
By 2016, most guiding services to Everest cost between US$35,000 and US$200,000.
Jamling Tenzing Norgay stated in 2003 that wealthy inexperienced climbers routinely summit Everest, endangering others and losing the spirit of adventure.
Shriya Shah-Klorfine died from oxygen deprivation during descent after climbing for 27 hours straight in 2012.
Reinhold Messner stated in 2004 that commercial Everest expeditions provide prepared routes, oxygen, meals, and bed setup.
By 2015, Nepal was considering requiring climbers have prior experience to improve mountain safety.
Four men summited Everest in one week in 2025 using xenon gas inhalation 10 days before expedition.
Sudarshan Gautam, a Canadian double-amputee, summited Everest in 2013.
Mount Everest has two main climbing routes: the Southeast Ridge from Nepal and North Ridge from Tibet.
Southeast Ridge Base Camp sits at 5,380 metres on Everest's south side in Nepal
The Khumbu Icefall is dangerous due to seracs, crevasses, and shifting ice blocks that have killed many climbers
Hillary and Tenzing first ascended the Hillary Step at 8,790 metres in 1953 using primitive climbing equipment
The Hillary Step has become a bottleneck with increasing climbers waiting for turns on fixed ropes
The Cornice traverse is the most exposed section with 2,400 metre drop to Southwest Face and 3,050 metres to Kangshung Face
North Ridge route begins in Tibet with base camp at 5,180 metres on Rongbuk Glacier's gravel plain
The Chinese ladder at Second Step was placed in 1975 by Chinese climbers and has been almost continuously in place since.
Mount Everest's summit rock is Ordovician limestone; the summit is capped with snow over ice.
Atmospheric pressure at Everest's summit is about one-third of sea-level pressure (337 mbar), providing only one-third as much oxygen.
An unacclimatized person exposed to atmospheric conditions above 8,500 m would likely lose consciousness within two to three minutes.
As of 2015, over 200 bodies remain on Mount Everest because injured climbers cannot be rescued and are typically left behind.
High altitude cerebral oedema commonly presents during descent from the summit; retinal haemorrhages can cause blindness in climbers.
About 95% of Everest summitters use bottled oxygen; about 5% have summitted without supplemental oxygen.
The death rate is double for climbers attempting Everest summit without supplemental oxygen.
Reinhold Messner and Peter Habeler made the first successful Everest climb without supplemental oxygen in 1978.
Messner summitted Everest solo in 1980 without supplemental oxygen, porters, or climbing partners via the northwest route.
Low oxygen at high altitudes impairs cognition and causes bradypsychia; climbers may suffer permanent neurological damage despite returning to lower altitudes.
Jon Krakauer's 1997 book criticized bottled oxygen for allowing unqualified climbers to summit and proposed banning it except for emergencies.
Mount Everest winter climbing is unpopular because of extreme cold, high winds reaching 270 km/h, and shorter days limiting climbing time.
Climbers have reported life-threatening thefts of gear, food, and oxygen bottles from supply caches and high-altitude camps on Mount Everest.
By the end of 2010, approximately 5,104 ascents to Mount Everest's summit had been completed by about 3,142 different individuals.
Reinhold Messner and Peter Habeler achieved the first ascent of Mount Everest without supplemental oxygen in 1978.
Jean-Marc Boivin completed the first paraglider descent of Mount Everest on September 26, 1988.
Didier Delsalle landed a helicopter on Mount Everest's summit in May 2005, setting world records for highest rotorcraft landing and take-off.
Lakpa Tsheri Sherpa and Sano Babu Sunuwar first completed a continuous summit-to-sea descent from Everest by paragliding to Namche Bazaar in 42 minutes.
Sano Babu Sunuwar had never previously climbed, and Lakpa Tsheri Sherpa had never kayaked and could not swim.
In 2014, mountaineer Wang Jing used a helicopter from South Base Camp to Camp II to avoid the Khumbu Icefall, then climbed to Everest summit.
In 2016, increased helicopter use over the Khumbu Icefall saved porters eighty trips while improving efficiency in material transport.
Yuichiro Miura became the first person to ski down Everest in the 1970s, descending nearly 1,300 vertical metres from the South Col.
The southern part of Mount Everest is regarded as one of several "hidden valleys" of refuge designated by the ninth-century Buddhist saint Padmasambhava.
In 2015, the president of the Nepal Mountaineering Association warned that Mount Everest's pollution, especially human waste, reached critical levels.
Approximately 12,000 kg of human excrement is left behind on Mount Everest each climbing season.
Human excrement is strewn across Mount Everest's summit route verges and the mountain's four south-side sleeping areas.
For 62 years of climbing on Mount Everest, climbers buried excrement in snow holes, slung it into crevasses, or defecated near tents.
Nepal's government requires climbers to pack out eight kilograms of waste when descending Mount Everest.
In February 2019, China closed its Mount Everest base camp to visitors without climbing permits due to mounting waste.
Mount Everest's waste problem includes spent oxygen tanks, abandoned tents, empty cans, and bottles alongside human excrement.
